description:
You are acting as a junior network-operations engineer who has just received a raw ICMP test capture from an on-site technician.  
The capture is already stored in the workstation at  

  /home/user/network/ping_results.txt  

Your assignment is to create a concise CSV report that management can paste straight into an internal dashboard.

1. Parse **only** the statistics sections of every host found in /home/user/network/ping_results.txt (the sections that look like “\<n\> packets transmitted, \<m\> received, \<x\>% packet loss, …”).  
2. For each host, extract the following four fields in this exact order:  
   • IP (as it appears on the “--- \<IP\> ping statistics ---” line)  
   • Lost (number of packets lost)  
   • Sent (number of packets transmitted)  
   • LossPercent (integer percentage, *without* the “%” symbol)  
   Loss = Sent − Received.
3. Write the results—**including a single header line**—to  

  /home/user/network/loss_report.csv  

4. Sort the data lines (not the header) alphabetically by the IP address string.
5. Make sure the file meets these formatting requirements so automated tests can validate it:  
   • Header must be exactly:  
     IP,Lost,Sent,LossPercent  
   • Each subsequent line must follow **precisely** the pattern:  
     \<IP\>,\<Lost\>,\<Sent\>,\<LossPercent\>  
   • Use a single UNIX newline character “\n” after every line, including the last line.  
   • Do **not** include any spaces, tabs, or extra columns.  
6. Use no more than two or three standard GNU command-line utilities in total (e.g., any combination of grep, awk, sed, cut, sort, uniq, tr, tail, head, etc.). A pipeline counts as multiple commands; the whole job should be solvable with at most three utilities strung together.
7. When you’re done, display (cat) the full contents of /home/user/network/loss_report.csv to the terminal so the reviewer can immediately see the result.

A correct solution will:
• Create the file at the exact location with correct name and permissions inherited from the user’s shell (no root needed).  
• Contain a header plus **exactly one line per host** that appears in the input file.  
• Show the lines in alphabetical order, header first.  
• Omit any additional commentary, blank lines, or percentage symbols.

Example target structure:

IP,Lost,Sent,LossPercent
10.0.0.1,2,2,100
172.16.0.1,1,2,50
192.168.1.1,0,2,0

Follow the above specification precisely so the automated grader can verify your work.
truth:
Initial workspace (already present before the agent starts):

Directory: /home/user/network
File: /home/user/network/ping_results.txt
Contents:
PING 192.168.1.1 (192.168.1.1) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 192.168.1.1: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.123 ms
64 bytes from 192.168.1.1: icmp_seq=2 ttl=64 time=0.112 ms

--- 192.168.1.1 ping statistics ---
2 packets transmitted, 2 received, 0% packet loss, time 1001ms

PING 10.0.0.1 (10.0.0.1) 56(84) bytes of data.
From 10.0.0.100 icmp_seq=1 Destination Host Unreachable
From 10.0.0.100 icmp_seq=2 Destination Host Unreachable

--- 10.0.0.1 ping statistics ---
2 packets transmitted, 0 received, 100% packet loss, time 2ms

PING 172.16.0.1 (172.16.0.1) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 172.16.0.1: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=1.345 ms
From 172.16.0.1 icmp_seq=2 Destination Host Unreachable

--- 172.16.0.1 ping statistics ---
2 packets transmitted, 1 received, 50% packet loss, time 2002ms

Expected artifact the agent must create:

File: /home/user/network/loss_report.csv
Exact byte-for-byte content (including final newline):
IP,Lost,Sent,LossPercent
10.0.0.1,2,2,100
172.16.0.1,1,2,50
192.168.1.1,0,2,0

A single trailing newline (ASCII 0x0A) follows the last “0”. No other files should be modified or created.
name: task_000000_14868175
